title: Rigid heddle pick-up table linen
subtitle: Weaving a simple supplementary-warp pattern for table napkins or placemats
excerpt: Use a pick-up stick on a rigid heddle loom to create a supplementary warp pattern across the body of cotton or linen napkins. The technique produces a subtle woven check or geometric design in a single colour without changing yarns.
sourceNotes: Synthesised from rigid-heddle pick-up weaving practice. Supplementary-warp patterns for table linen are documented in Jane Patrick, The Weaver's Idea Book (2010, in-copyright, substance only referenced), and in British guild rigid-heddle teaching.

glossaryTerms.definition: A flat stick threaded through the slot threads of a rigid-heddle warp to create a third shed. Turning the stick on its edge while the heddle is in neutral position opens a patterned shed inaccessible by the heddle alone.
The number of warp threads per unit of width. Table linen in 10/2 cotton works well at 20 to 24 epi on a 8-dent heddle with two threads per slot or per hole.
The threads stretched lengthways on the loom. In pick-up work, selected warp threads are lifted with the stick to create pattern floats on the face of the cloth.

content.text: opens a third shed that neither the heddle-up nor heddle-down position can produce. For table linen, this third shed is used every few picks to raise selected
threads in a repeating pattern, creating a subtle geometric texture in the cloth surface. At the
used for table linen (20 to 24 epi in 10/2 cotton), the pattern reads as a light woven check or spot that becomes more distinct after washing and pressing.

items.name: 10/2 cotton or linen yarn, white or natural
Rigid heddle loom, minimum 35 cm weaving width

content.text: Step 1: Warp and thread the loom

Warp the rigid heddle loom with 10/2 cotton at 20 epi in an 8-dent heddle (one thread per hole and one per slot, doubled for 20 epi). For four napkins at 35 cm wide, warp to 37 cm (allowing 2 cm draw-in) and a total warp length of 250 cm.
Step 2: Set up the pick-up pattern

With the heddle in the neutral (centre) position, slide the pick-up stick under alternating groups of slot threads across the warp: pick up 4 threads, skip 4, pick up 4, skip 4, across the full width. Leave the stick in the warp between picks; turn it on its edge to open the pattern shed.

The treadling sequence for a simple pick-up spot: heddle up, pass shuttle; heddle down, pass shuttle; pick-up shed (stick on edge), pass shuttle; heddle down, pass shuttle. Repeat this four-pick sequence throughout the body of the napkin. The pick-up pick fires every fourth row, creating a repeating spot pattern across the cloth.

Cut the napkins apart, leaving 2 cm of warp at each end for a hemmed edge. Machine-stitch or hand-hem the warp ends, or hemstitch before cutting from the loom. Wash and press with a hot iron while still damp for a crisp finish.

items.problem: The pick-up pattern is not visible in the finished cloth
items.cause: The pick-up shed is not firing cleanly, or the stick is not on edge when throwing the shuttle
items.fix: Check that the stick is fully on its edge before throwing the shuttle, opening a clear shed. The pattern pick must lift the selected threads clearly above the others.

items.problem: The cloth is stiff rather than soft and supple after washing
items.cause: The sett is too close for the cotton grist
items.fix: A sett of 20 epi in 10/2 cotton should produce a flexible cloth. If stiff, the selvedges were drawn in too tightly; reduce warp ends slightly on the next project.
